Probability & Statistics · Part 2

Distributions, Expectation & the Continuous Leap Random variables → the discrete distribution family → continuous variables, variance & LOTUS

A complete, self-contained reference for everything covered in this session. Every concept leads with intuition, carries its full math, and ends with worked problems (question first) and key takeaways. Built to read offline, forever.

What we covered

  1. Random variables & random processes
  2. Bernoulli distribution
  3. Binomial distribution
  4. Probability mass function (PMF)
  5. Indicator random variables
  6. Hypergeometric distribution
  7. Cumulative distribution function (CDF)
  8. Expected value & its properties
  9. Geometric distribution
  10. Negative binomial distribution
  11. Poisson distribution
  12. Binomial → Poisson limit
  13. Probability density function (PDF)
  14. Continuous expectation
  15. Variance & standard deviation
  16. Uniform distribution
  17. LOTUS
  18. Master cheat-sheet

01Random Variables & Random Processes

Simple story An experiment produces messy outcomes — HHT, "satisfied", a pair of dice faces. You can't average "satisfied" or square HHT. A random variable is a rule that attaches a number to every outcome, translating "stuff that happened" into "a number describing it" so you can finally do math.

Definition

A random variable is a function

where is the sample space (all possible raw outcomes) and is the real line. Plain English: a random variable reads an outcome and reports a number summarizing the part you care about.

Why we need it

  • You care about a number, not the full outcome. Flipping 3 coins, you want "how many heads," not the exact sequence. "number of heads" discards ordering and keeps the summary.
  • Math needs numbers. Expectation, variance, every operation is arithmetic.
  • It compresses the sample space. 8 raw outcomes of 3 flips collapse to 4 values (0,1,2,3).

The mapping is fixed; the input is random

Flip a fair coin twice, , and let number of heads:

Key ideaThe function is deterministic. The randomness lives in which outcome occurs, not in the mapping. The function is fixed; the input is random.

Notation: "" is secretly a set of outcomes

We write to denote the event — the set of outcomes that map to 1:

For the coin example, is the event . So asks "what's the probability of the set of outcomes producing the value 1?" The is the preimage: "work backwards from the number to the outcomes."

Random processes (one draw vs. a movie of draws)

A random variable gives one number from one experiment. A random process (stochastic process) is a whole family of random variables, usually indexed by time or step:

One random variable is a snapshot; a random process is a movie. A random walk is the canonical example: each step flips a coin, your position after steps is , and the sequence is the process.

Where it shows up in ML/DSTime series (prices, sensors, demand), Markov chains (PageRank, RL state transitions, MCMC). For now: a random variable is one draw; a random process is a sequence of draws evolving over an index.

02Bernoulli Distribution

Simple storyThe simplest non-trivial random variable: a single yes/no. One coin flip, one click-or-not, one spam-or-not. Two outcomes coded as 1 (success) and 0 (failure).

Definition

has the Bernoulli distribution if it takes only values 0 and 1, with some such that

written . The symbol reads "is distributed as." Here is the probability of "success" (which event counts as success is your modeling choice), and is everything else.

Where it shows up in ML/DS — this is not a toy
  • Logistic regression outputs a probability and models the label as . Binary cross-entropy is the Bernoulli likelihood — that's why it's the loss.
  • Any binary classification target is Bernoulli per example.
  • Dropout: each neuron is kept with prob , dropped with — a Bernoulli mask per neuron.
  • A/B testing: whether one user converts is a Bernoulli trial.
Interview thread"Why is binary cross-entropy the loss for logistic regression?" → because the label is Bernoulli-distributed and we maximize its likelihood. (Closes fully when we hit MLE.)

03Binomial Distribution

Simple storyBernoulli is one flip. Binomial is: do independent identical Bernoulli flips and count the successes. 3 flips count heads; 100 ad-views count clicks; the count is binomial.

Definition

The number of successes in independent trials:

written .

Reading the formula

"Probability of exactly successes = (ways to arrange successes among slots) × (probability of any one such arrangement)."

  • — successes, each prob , multiplied (independence).
  • — the remaining must be failures.
  • — counts the arrangements; all share the same probability, so multiply.
Assumptions & failure modesBinomial needs (1) fixed decided in advance, (2) independent trials, (3) same . Break them: changing (fatigue) → not binomial; dependent draws (no replacement) → hypergeometric; stop at first success → geometric.
Where it shows up in ML/DSA/B testing (conversions out of N), confidence intervals around a click rate or accuracy (sampling distribution of ), and reasoning about whether 92% accuracy beats 90% or is just noise on a small test set.
Relationship to lock in is a sum of independent , and . Bernoulli is the atom; binomial is the molecule.
Worked problem 3.1A fair coin is flipped 5 times. What is the probability of getting exactly 3 heads?
Step 1 — identify

Fixed , independent, same , count successes → , want .

Step 2 — PMF
Step 3 — coefficient
Step 4 — probability pieces
Step 5 — multiply
Answer: 0.3125, about 31.25%.
Worked problem 3.2An ad has a true click-through rate of 20%. It is shown to 8 independent users. What is the probability that exactly 2 click?
Step 1 — identify

, want .

Step 2 — PMF
Step 3 — coefficient
Step 4 — pieces
Step 5 — multiply
Answer: about 0.294, or 29.4%.
Worked problem 3.3 (conceptual)Show that a single Bernoulli equals Bin(1, p) by plugging into the binomial PMF.
Step 1 — set n=1
Step 2 — at k=1
Step 3 — at k=0
Step 4 — compare

We recovered , — exactly Bernoulli.

Conclusion: . Bernoulli is the atomic case.

04Probability Mass Function (PMF)

Simple storyFor a discrete random variable (values you can list: 0,1,2,…), the PMF is the function giving the probability of each possible value — the complete "menu" of the variable.

Definition

Feed it a value ; it returns the probability equals exactly that. The subscript labels which variable's PMF it is.

The "mass" imagePicture 1 kg of probability. The PMF says how much sits on each value. Discrete variables pile mass at points; continuous variables (later) smear it into a density — which is why those get a PDF, not a PMF.

Two properties every PMF must satisfy

Probabilities can't be negative; all the mass adds to the full kilogram (the variable takes some value).

The PMFs of our distributions, explicit

For :

For :

Key ideaThe "distribution formula" and the "PMF" are the same object: the distribution is the concept, the PMF is the explicit function.

05Indicator Random Variables

Simple storyThe simplest possible random variable: a switch answering one yes/no question with 1 or 0. "Did this specific thing happen?" It indicates whether an event occurred.

Definition

An indicator is a . "Indicator" stresses its role (flagging an event); "Bernoulli" stresses its distribution. Same object.

The key trick: binomial as a sum of indicators

where each is i.i.d. . i.i.d. = independent and identically distributed. Each contributes a 1 exactly when trial succeeds, so the sum is the count of successes.

Why it's so useful — linearity of expectation A single indicator's mean is . Expectation is linear (sum of expectations = expectation of sum, even under dependence), so
You just derived in one line. Decompose the count into 1/0 switches, take each expectation, add.
Where it shows up in ML/DS
  • Accuracy is a mean of indicators: if prediction correct → accuracy . Precision, recall, hit rate, conversion rate — all indicator averages.
  • 0/1 loss is an indicator (1 if misclassified).
  • One-hot encoding is a vector of indicators.
  • Counting via expectation: expected collisions, expected users who do X — write as a sum of indicators, use linearity.

Two quick results

The binomial PMF sums to 1

with , so and . Some number of successes definitely happens; the binomial theorem makes it automatic.

Sum of independent same- binomials is binomial

Story proof: pool flips and flips at the same into flips. The must match.

Worked problem 5.1A 52-card deck is shuffled. What is the expected number of aces in a 5-card hand? Solve it the indicator way.
Step 1 — indicators

if card is an ace. .

Step 2 — single position

By symmetry any drawn card is an ace with prob , so .

Step 3 — linearity (despite dependence)
Answer: aces. Indicators + linearity gave the mean despite without-replacement dependence — no full hypergeometric PMF needed.

06Hypergeometric Distribution

Simple storyBinomial counts successes when sampling with replacement (independent, fixed ). Hypergeometric counts successes when sampling without replacement from a finite pool — once you pull an item, it's gone, so the odds shift. The trials are dependent; that's the whole difference.

Setup & definition

white and black marbles; draw without replacement; number of white drawn:

written , valid for and .

Reading the formula — favorable over total

  • — ways to choose whites from .
  • — ways to choose the remaining blacks from ; multiply since both must happen.
  • — total ways to draw any (the full sample space).
Why it isn't binomialWithout replacement, changes trial to trial and draws are coupled. The instant you hear "without replacement from a finite pool," switch off binomial and switch on hypergeometric.

The card example (X = #aces in a 5-card hand)

Aces are "white" (), non-aces "black" (), draw :

It's a valid PMF (Vandermonde)

Where it shows upReal life: QC without replacement, card/lottery odds, capture-recapture. ML/DS: gene set enrichment analysis (sampling genes without replacement) and Fisher's exact test for small 2×2 tables — both hypothesis tests, the on-ramp to inferential statistics.
Binomial ⇄ hypergeometricWhen the population is huge vs. the sample (rule of thumb ~20×), removing a few items barely shifts proportions, so hypergeometric ≈ with . This is why pollsters treat "1000 from a billion" as binomial.
Worked problem 6.1A box has 12 bulbs, 4 defective. You pick 3 without replacement. P(exactly 1 defective)?
Step 1 — identify

Without replacement → , want .

Step 2 — PMF
Step 3 — pieces
Step 4 — combine
Answer: , about 51%.
Worked problem 6.2 (judgment)10,000 examples, 3,000 positive. Sample 100 without replacement for validation. Is the positive count binomial or hypergeometric — and does it matter?
Step 1 — strictly

Without replacement from a finite labeled pool → .

Step 2 — approximation

Population (10,000) is 100× the sample — far above the ~20× rule. Removing 100 barely shifts the 30%.

Step 3 — conclude

Treat as . Means are identical (); hypergeometric variance is only slightly smaller (finite-population correction).

Answer: technically hypergeometric, but binomial is an excellent approximation when the population dwarfs the sample.

07Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)

Simple storyThe PMF answers "probability equals exactly this?" The CDF answers "probability is at most this?" Walk the number line left to right, scooping up probability mass. At any point, the CDF is how much you've scooped.

Definition

Discrete: a staircase

Flat between values, jumps at each value, jump size = the PMF there:

Continuous: a smooth rising curve

No jumps; mass is smeared, so the CDF integrates the density:

Discrete sums, continuous integratesThe CDF is the one notation that works for both worlds. The PMF exists only for discrete, the PDF only for continuous, but is universal.

The three properties of a valid CDF

  1. Monotonically nondecreasing — a running total only adds mass, never removes it.
  2. Right-continuous — the "" includes the point , so a jump's mass is counted at (step drawn filled at the top).
  3. Limits to 0 and 1:
Why the CDF earns its keepInterval probability ; it's universal across discrete/continuous; and inverse-transform sampling inverts the CDF to turn uniform randoms into samples from any distribution (under the hood of np.random).

Independence (now for random variables)

Discrete equivalent: . Knowing tells you nothing about ; the joint factors into the product.

Worked problem 7.1PMF: P(X=0)=0.2, P(X=1)=0.5, P(X=2)=0.3. Write the full CDF and find P(0 < X ≤ 2).
Step 1 — accumulate
Step 2 — validity

Nondecreasing 0→0.2→0.7→1.0, ends at 1. Valid.

Step 3 — interval via CDF

Cross-check: . (Subtracting removes the mass the strict "" excludes.)

Answer: the staircase above; .

08Expected Value & Its Properties

Simple storyThe expected value is the long-run average — the distribution's center of mass. Balance the PMF on a pivot and the pivot sits at . It need not be an attainable value: the expected heads in one fair flip is 0.5. "Expected" means "average," not "anticipated."

Definition (discrete)

A probability-weighted average: each value times its probability, summed. is the set of values can take.

The weighted-average intuition

Average of regrouped by value:

That is the expectation formula — the average with probabilities as weights.

Bernoulli & the indicator bridge

If is the indicator of event , then so

Probability ⇄ expectationTo find the probability of an event, compute the expected value of its indicator. This is why "average of indicators" = "probability of the event" (accuracy = average correctness = probability of being correct).

Linearity of expectation (the most important property)

The magicLinearity holds even when and are dependent. No independence needed. (Variance and are not like this.)

Why it's true

Splitting a sum into two needs no assumption about how relate — the joint structure never enters.

Payoff: in one line

And under dependence — expected aces in a 5-card hand:

Why you need expected values
  • The one-number summary of a distribution's center; decisions ride on averages (expected revenue, loss, latency).
  • Every ML objective: training minimizes expected loss (risk). Empirical risk = average loss = empirical expectation. loss.mean() is an expectation.
  • Variance, covariance (PCA), entropy/cross-entropy are all expectations.
  • RL: the value of a state is expected cumulative reward.

09Geometric Distribution

Simple storyKeep doing independent Bernoulli trials until the first success, and count the failures before it. "How many times do I fail before I finally succeed?" How many tails before the first head; how many rejections before the first offer.

Definition (and a convention warning)

This source counts failures before the first success. For :

Two conventions existThis one counts failures before success (starts at 0). The other counts the trial number of the first success (starts at 1). They differ by 1. scipy.stats.geom uses the trial-number version. Always check.

Reading the PMF

failures in a row () then one success (). No binomial coefficient — only one arrangement works (fail,…,fail,succeed); the order is forced.

Valid PMF (geometric series → hence the name)

Mean

Rare success (small ) → many failures first. failure; .

First-step analysis (the technique that generalizes to RL) Condition on the first trial: success (prob ) → 0 failures; failure (prob ) → 1 failure, then back to start ().
"Condition on the first step, write a recursion, solve" is exactly how Markov-chain / Bellman value computations work.

Memorylessness — the defining property

The only discrete distribution that is memoryless. Past failures carry no information about the future — which is why "I'm due for a win" is a fallacy and why first-step analysis is so clean.

Where it shows up in ML/DS
  • Retry/waiting counts (network retries, rejection sampling): expected attempts drives capacity planning.
  • Rejection sampling efficiency: proposals per accepted sample is geometric; low acceptance → slow sampler.
  • RL discounting: if an episode continues with prob each step, the horizon is geometric with mean ; the discounted return is an expectation over a geometric horizon.
  • Churn/survival: periods until churn (constant per-period rate); discrete cousin of the exponential.
Worked problem 9.1A salesperson closes each call with p=0.2 (failures-before-first-success convention). Expected failures before the first close? P(exactly 3 failures then a close)?
Step 1 — identify

, .

Step 2 — mean
Step 3 — P(X=3)
Answer: 4 expected failures; ~10.2% chance of exactly 3 failures before the first close. (So on average the 5th call is the first close — consistent with a 1-in-5 rate.)

10Negative Binomial Distribution

Simple storyThe geometric's bigger sibling: count the failures until the th success (geometric waits for 1). "How many failures before my 3rd success?" Set and it collapses back to geometric.

Definition

written .

Reading the PMF — why the coefficient looks odd

  • — successes; — failures.
  • — the last trial must be the th success (that's what stops the process), so it's pinned. Arrange the other successes among the first slots.

If the last trial were a failure, the th success already happened earlier and you'd have stopped. With , — recovering the geometric's missing coefficient.

Mean (via decomposition into geometrics)

Each is the failures between consecutive successes (memorylessness makes them i.i.d.). By linearity:

Waiting for successes takes about times as long as waiting for 1.

Killer use — overdispersed countsPoisson assumes mean = variance. Real count data (purchases/customer, claims, visits) often has variance ≫ mean. Then you swap Poisson regression for negative binomial regression. RNA-seq tools (DESeq2, edgeR) model overdispersed read counts as negative binomial. Compression: negative binomial = the count distribution when Poisson's mean=variance breaks.
Worked problem 10.1A player makes each free throw with p=0.7. P(exactly 2 misses before the 3rd make)? Expected misses before the 3rd make?
Step 1 — identify

Failures before the th success → , , want .

Step 2 — PMF
Step 3 — pieces
Step 4 — combine
Step 5 — mean
Answer: ~0.185 (18.5%) for exactly 2 misses before the 3rd make; expected misses ≈ 1.29.
The family, one sentenceFix one trial (Bernoulli) · fix , count successes (binomial) · count to first success (geometric) · count to th success (negative binomial) · rare events over an interval (Poisson, next).

11Poisson Distribution

Simple storyCount how many times a rare event happens in a fixed interval (time, space) when there are many opportunities, each individually unlikely. Emails per hour, earthquakes per year, typos per page, chips in a cookie.

Definition

written . The parameter is the rate: the average number of occurrences in the interval.

Valid PMF (Taylor series of )

Mean (and the signature fact)

Signature propertyFor Poisson, mean = variance = . This equality is the Poisson's fingerprint — and exactly the assumption that breaks under overdispersion (sending you to the negative binomial).
Where it shows up in ML/DS
  • Poisson regression (a core GLM) when the target is a count (purchases, clicks, claims).
  • Overdispersion bridge: when variance ≫ mean, upgrade to negative binomial.
  • Queueing / systems: request arrivals (M/M/1 queues) — relevant for ML systems design, throughput, tail latency.
  • Anomaly detection: Poisson baseline for rare-event counts per window; deviations from flag anomalies.

Sum of Poissons

Rates just add.

12The Binomial → Poisson Limit

Simple storyThe Poisson isn't a separate invention — it's what the binomial becomes when you have a huge number of trials, each with a tiny success probability, holding the average count fixed. Same phenomenon, different scale.

The precise statement

Three conditions together: many trials (), tiny per-trial probability (), fixed average (). Plain English: rare event, many chances, fixed rate → Poisson.

Why it works (the limit)

Substitute into the binomial PMF and take the limit. Three pieces survive:

The cancels, leaving:

Where the comes fromThe single step to remember: . That limit is the birthplace of the in every Poisson formula.

When to invoke it (the cases)

  • large (rule of thumb , ideally ),
  • small (),
  • moderate.
Why bother(1) The Poisson PMF (just and ) avoids gigantic factorials like . (2) Often you only know the rate , not and separately — exactly the real-world situation for arrivals, defects, rare events.
BinomialPoisson
You knowtrials and per-trial only the rate over an interval
Supportbounded, unbounded,
Mean
Variance as (= mean)

As the binomial's mean and variance both approach — the approximation literally drags them together into the Poisson's mean=variance signature.

Worked problem 12.1A factory has a 0.5% defect rate. In a batch of 400, P(exactly 3 defective)? Use the Poisson approximation.
Step 1 — conditions & rate

large, small → .

Step 2 — PMF at k=3
Step 3 — compute
Answer: ~0.180 (18%). Exact binomial ≈ 0.1805 — the approximation is excellent.

★Three Famous Worked Examples

These three recur in interviews and each teaches a transferable move: linearity beats dependence, the mean can mislead, and the Poisson approximation tames brutal combinatorics.

Example A — Local maxima (indicators + symmetry)Shuffle 1..n (n ≥ 2) into a random order. A position is a local maximum if it exceeds both neighbors (endpoints need only beat their single neighbor). Find the expected number of local maxima.
Step 0 — why indicators

The count has overlapping, dependent conditions. Linearity of expectation ignores dependence, so define an indicator per position and sum.

Step 1 — interior positions

Look at the 3 values at positions . Only their relative rank matters; by symmetry the largest is in the center with probability .

Step 2 — endpoints

Only one neighbor; the endpoint is the larger of 2 with probability (2 endpoints).

Step 3 — sum with linearity
Answer: . Check : gives 1, and with two numbers the larger is always the single local max. Lesson: "max of items in a specific slot" has probability .
Example B — St. Petersburg paradox (when the mean misleads)Flip a fair coin until the first heads; if heads first appears on flip X, you win $2^X$. What is the fair price (expected payout)?
Step 1 — distribution of X

First success on flip (counting the success): ,

Step 2 — expected payout
Step 3 — each term

The doubling payout and halving probability cancel exactly — every outcome contributes $1.

Answer: . By "fair price = expected value" you'd pay anything, yet nobody would. Expected value isn't always the right decision rule — this motivates expected utility & risk-sensitive objectives. A finite payout cap makes the fair price finite (~ dollars).
Example C — Birthday triple match (Poisson approximation)With n people and 365 equally likely birthdays, approximate the probability that at least three people share a birthday.
Step 1 — indicators over triples

There are triples; if all three share a birthday.

Step 2 — one triple's probability

Anchor on person 's birthday; the other two must match it.

Step 3 — expected matches (linearity)
Step 4 — approximate as Poisson

Many triples, each rare, weakly dependent → .

Step 5 — at least one
Answer: . The exact inclusion-exclusion is a nightmare; the Poisson approximation sidesteps it. The "probability at least one rare event" form is a reusable workhorse.

13Probability Density Function (PDF)

Simple storyContinuous variables (exact height, wait time, temperature) can take any value in a range, so there are infinitely many — and the probability of any exact value is zero. The PMF idea collapses. Instead we talk about probability density around a point and get real probabilities by integrating over a range. Area under the curve = probability.

Definition

The probability falls in is the area under the density there. The PDF itself isn't a probability; its area is.

Two properties of a valid PDF

PDF values are NOT probabilitiesA PDF can exceed 1 at a point (e.g. has height 2) as long as the total area is 1. Only the integral is a probability. This trips people up constantly.

The crucial intuition: density × width ≈ probability

Height × small width ≈ probability of that little window. The PDF is the rate at which probability accumulates — like physical density (mass per unit length), not mass itself. High density = values cluster there.

PDF ⇄ CDF (the bridge)

Integrate the PDF to get the CDF; differentiate the CDF to get the PDF (Fundamental Theorem of Calculus). Also . For continuous variables and agree (single points have zero probability).

14Continuous Expectation

Simple storySame idea as discrete — a probability-weighted average — but since we can't sum individual values, we integrate. Value times density, integrated.

Definition

The one translation that carries everything
Sum → integral, PMF → PDF, everything else unchanged. This single substitution carries over to variance and LOTUS. Still the center of mass; still silent about spread (variance fills that gap next).

15Variance & Standard Deviation

Simple storyThe mean gives the center; two distributions with the same center can look utterly different — one tight, one wild. Variance measures spread: how far values typically sit from the mean. Small = predictable; large = volatile.

Why we need it

"Average return 8%" describes both a steady bond and a wild bet that's usually −50% or +66%. The mean hides risk; variance exposes it. In ML: the bias–variance tradeoff, gradient noise, prediction uncertainty, metric volatility across runs.

Definition

The average squared distance from the mean.

Why squared, not the alternatives
  • Why not ? It's always 0 — positive and negative deviations cancel:
    Squaring kills the cancellation.
  • Why not ? It's valid (mean absolute deviation) but absolute values aren't differentiable at 0 and are algebraically ugly. Squaring is smooth and plays beautifully with calculus and linearity.

The computational form (the one you use)

The mean of the squares minus the square of the mean. Derivation via linearity (treat as the constant it is):

Standard deviation

Variance is in squared units (dollars²); the square root returns interpretable original units — the "typical distance from the mean." Variance for math, SD for human-readable spread.

Variance is NOT linear (constant comes out squared), and only if independent. Sharp contrast with expectation, whose linearity needed no independence.

16Uniform Distribution

Simple storyA completely random point in , no part favored. The PDF is flat — a rectangle. The continuous analog of a fair die.

Definition & finding the constant

A PDF integrates to 1, and the area is a rectangle of width and height :

Density = 1 / (interval length). Wider interval → shorter rectangle. (The height can exceed 1 — the concrete "PDF isn't a probability" case.)

Expected value & variance

Variance via :

Variance grows with the square of the width: double the interval, quadruple the variance. (This appears in quantization-error analysis.)

Where it shows up in MLRandom weight init (He/Glorot uniform); the bedrock of sampling (RNGs produce first, then transform via inverse-CDF); uninformative Bayesian priors; dropout masks, random crops, augmentation positions.
Worked problem 16.1A bus arrives uniformly at random between 10:00 and 10:30. You arrive at 10:00. Expected wait and its standard deviation?
Step 1 — model

minutes.

Step 2 — mean
Step 3 — variance
Step 4 — SD
Answer: expected wait 15 min, SD ≈ 8.66 min (in minutes — the reason we take the root of variance).

17LOTUS — Law of the Unconscious Statistician

Simple storyYou know . But often you want the average of a function of — , , . LOTUS: don't bother finding the distribution of — just weight by 's own density/mass and sum/integrate.

Definition

Exactly as you put it: ordinary expectation has times the density; LOTUS just swaps the for . The weights stay untouched.

Why "unconscious"Statisticians apply it so reflexively (writing without a thought) that they use it unconsciously — forgetting it's a theorem. The non-obvious part it skips: you'd think you need the distribution of , but you can shortcut straight through 's distribution.
Why it's a true generalization — the engine of everything
  • Variance: is LOTUS with — every variance you computed used it.
  • Higher moments (skew, kurtosis): .
  • Moment generating functions (used to prove the CLT).
  • Expected loss / risk — averaging loss over data is empirical LOTUS.
  • Entropy and KL divergence.
gives one number; LOTUS unlocks for any — the whole toolkit of moments, risk, and information theory.
Worked problem 17.1PDF f(x)=2x for 0 ≤ x ≤ 1 (else 0). Find E[X] and Var(X).
Step 1 — valid PDF?
Step 2 — E[X]
Step 3 — E[X²] via LOTUS (g(x)=x²)
Step 4 — variance
Answer: , . Step 3 was LOTUS in action — swapped for , density untouched.

18Master Cheat-Sheet

The discrete distribution family — what you fix and what you count

DistributionCountsPMFMeanKey trait
one trial: 0/1the atom
successes in fixed indep., fixed
whites drawn (no replacement)dependent draws
failures before 1st successmemoryless
failures before th successsum of geometrics; overdispersion
rare events per intervalmean = variance =
random point in (continuous)variance

Discrete vs. continuous — the same machinery, sum ↔ integral

ConceptDiscreteContinuous
Prob. functionPMF PDF
CDF
Expectation
Variance  (identical both worlds)
LOTUS

The five highest-leverage takeaways

Carry these into any interview
  1. Linearity of expectation ignores dependence. Decompose any count into indicators, take each mean, sum. This cracked , the card problems, local maxima, and the birthday triples.
  2. Continuous = discrete with sum → integral, PMF → PDF. Expectation, variance, LOTUS all transfer for free. The only genuinely new ideas: "probability is area, not height" and "PDF values aren't probabilities."
  3. The mean hides spread (and can mislead). Variance exists for this; St. Petersburg shows the mean alone can be a terrible decision rule.
  4. The discrete family is one idea separated by what you fix and what you count: Bernoulli → binomial → geometric → negative binomial → Poisson.
  5. LOTUS is the engine behind variance, moments, MGFs, entropy, and expected loss — the difference between "I can find the average" and "I can find the average of anything."
Traps to remember with replacement / fixed p → binomial without replacement → hypergeometric stop at 1st success → geometric rare + many + fixed rate → Poisson variance NOT linear; needs independence for sums geometric: two conventions, differ by 1 PDF can exceed 1; only its area is probability
Probability & Statistics — Part 2 · Distributions, Expectation & the Continuous Leap. Self-contained study reference; math rendered offline. Topics covered in order: random variables & processes · Bernoulli · binomial · PMF · indicators · hypergeometric · CDF · expectation & linearity · geometric · negative binomial · Poisson · binomial→Poisson · PDF · continuous expectation · variance & SD · uniform · LOTUS.
